comments:
Summary:
This paper addresses the sparsity/diagonal-dominance problems of graphlet kernels by treating normalized graphlet-frequency vectors as multinomial estimates and applying a structurally-aware smoothing scheme inspired by Kneser–Ney. The authors construct a DAG linking graphlets of size k to those of size k+1 (edge formed when deletion of one node in the larger yields the smaller) and use these links to define a base distribution for smoothing. They propose a Structural Kneser–Ney (SKN) estimator and a hierarchical Bayesian variant based on a modified Pitman–Yor process (PYP) that uses the DAG to transform draws between levels. Experiments on standard chemical/biological graph benchmarks show consistent improvements over plain graphlet MLE kernels and hash-kernel baselines, and competitive results versus other graph kernels.

Strengths:
- Addresses an important, well-motivated problem in graph kernels: how to avoid diagonal dominance when using large graphlets while preserving discriminative power.
- The idea of exploiting the inherent structure among graphlets (via the parent-child DAG) to define a principled fallback/base distribution is natural and elegant.
- Adapting Kneser–Ney and providing a Bayesian PYP extension is a solid and interesting methodological contribution.
- Experiments on multiple public datasets demonstrate practical gains and analyze sensitivity to discounting and graphlet size.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- Scalability and complexity: constructing the full DAG requires enumerating all non-isomorphic graphs up to size k (noted as one-time O(2^k) effort). The paper does not provide a clear discussion of the practical limits of k or the cost/memory of DAG construction for larger k; this is important since the proposed approach depends on these precomputed relationships.
- Edge-weighting choices: Equation (7) uses parent-occurrence counts to set child weights. While reasonable, the choice is heuristic and alternative weighting schemes are only briefly mentioned; more discussion or ablation on how sensitive results are to this choice would be useful.
- PYP hyperparameters and tuning: the PYP variant is promising but the authors report not tuning hyperparameters carefully and observe worse PYP results than SKN. This leaves the Bayesian claim under-explored—more thorough hyperparameter tuning or guidance is needed.
- Sampling vs. exhaustive counts: experiments use 10,000 sampled subgraphs per graph (as in earlier graphlet work). Sampling variance and its effect on the smoothed estimates are not deeply quantified (though an experiment with exhaustive counts on some datasets is presented). It would help to report variance/CI across repeated samplings or to show robustness to sampling budget.
- Baseline hyperparameter choices: the SVM regularization parameter C was fixed (C=1) and some kernel hyperparameters were set to single values; while this keeps comparisons controlled, it could under- or over-estimate baseline performance. A short discussion of this choice and its potential effect would be helpful.
- Theoretical analysis: beyond Lemma 1 and the connection to Kneser–Ney/PYP, there is limited theoretical analysis of when the structural smoothing provably helps (e.g., conditions on sparsity/power-law). Some more insight would strengthen the work.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Add a complexity/runtime table detailing: DAG construction time/memory per k, per-graph preprocessing (sampling & Nauty), and kernel computation times compared to baselines.
- Provide an ablation study on edge-weighting choices (e.g., uniform, parent-occurrence, PageRank-based) and sampling budgets to show sensitivity.
- Tune or at least describe procedures used to tune PYP hyperparameters; report results when PYP is carefully tuned or provide guidelines for practitioners.
- Report variability due to random sampling (e.g., mean ± std over multiple samplings) to demonstrate robustness.
- Expand discussion on limitations (e.g., max feasible k) and possible approximate alternatives for very large k.

Overall assessment and recommendation:
This is a well-motivated and solidly executed piece of work that brings smoothing techniques from language modeling into the graph kernel domain in a thoughtful, structure-aware way. The methodological contribution is clear and the empirical results are encouraging. The main gaps are in scalability discussion, sensitivity analyses (edge weights, PYP hyperparameters, sampling variance), and some practical guidance for users. With these clarifications and additional experiments, the paper would make a strong contribution to the graph-kernel literature. I recommend acceptance (highly suitable for presentation), with the suggestion to address the points above in revision or the final version.
